title: Quantum Repeaters Quiz
description: Test your understanding of quantum repeaters, entanglement swapping, and long-distance quantum communication.

questions.question: Why can't we use classical amplifiers to extend quantum communication range?
options: The no-cloning theorem prevents copying quantum states for amplification
Classical amplifiers add too much noise
Quantum signals are too weak for amplifiers
questions.explanation: Classical repeaters amplify by copying the signal. However, the no-cloning theorem forbids copying arbitrary quantum states. Any attempt to amplify would destroy the quantum information. This fundamental physics constraint necessitates quantum repeaters.

questions.question: What is the primary operation performed at a quantum repeater node?
questions.explanation: Quantum repeaters perform entanglement swapping: a Bell state measurement on two qubits from adjacent segments. This connects the entanglement from separate pairs without needing to transmit quantum states across the full distance.

questions.question: In entanglement swapping, if A-B and C-D are entangled pairs (with B and C at the same location), what happens after a Bell measurement on B and C?
options: A and D become entangled, while B and C are measured
B and C become more strongly entangled
questions.explanation: After the Bell measurement on B and C, these qubits collapse to definite states. Remarkably, A and D (which never interacted directly) become entangled. The measurement result tells us which Bell state A-D is in, allowing correction.

questions.question: What is the purpose of entanglement purification in quantum repeaters?
options: To improve the fidelity of entangled pairs by sacrificing some pairs
To extend the range of entanglement
To reduce the number of repeater nodes needed
questions.explanation: Purification takes multiple low-fidelity entangled pairs and produces fewer pairs with higher fidelity. It's essential because channel noise reduces fidelity, and this error accumulates over long distances. Purification trades quantity for quality.

questions.question: For a 1000 km link with 0.2 dB/km loss, approximately what is the direct transmission probability?
questions.explanation: Loss = 0.2 dB/km × 1000 km = 200 dB. P = 10^(-200/10) = 10^-20. This is essentially zero - you'd need to send 10^20 photons to get one through on average. This is why quantum repeaters are absolutely essential for long distances.

questions.question: What is the primary technical challenge for quantum repeaters?
options: Quantum memory with sufficient coherence time
questions.explanation: Quantum repeaters require storing quantum states while waiting for all segments to establish entanglement. If the memory decoheres before the protocol completes, the entanglement is lost. Developing long-coherence quantum memories is the main technological hurdle.

questions.question: How does increasing the number of repeater segments help with transmission probability?
options: It doesn't help - total loss is the same
Shorter segments have higher per-segment success, improving overall rate
It only helps if segments overlap
questions.explanation: With N segments of length L/N, each segment has much higher transmission probability (P^(1/N) vs P for direct). Even though you need success in all segments, with heralding and quantum memory, you can retry failed segments independently, dramatically improving overall rate.

questions.question: In the BBPSSW purification protocol, what happens when purification fails?
options: Both input pairs are lost and you must start over with new pairs
The pairs are returned to their original state
questions.explanation: Purification is probabilistic. When it fails, both input pairs are destroyed by the measurement. You must generate new entangled pairs and try again. This is why purification trades quantity for quality - you sacrifice pairs for higher fidelity.
